text:
HEAD AND NECK ONCOLOGY MULTIDISCIPLINARY CONSULT NOTE

ATTENDING: [Head and Neck Medical Oncologist, Multidisciplinary Team]
SERVICE: Head and Neck Oncology / Tumor Board Consultation
REFERRING PROVIDER: [ENT/Otolaryngology]

---

CHIEF COMPLAINT
Newly diagnosed squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue; presenting for initial multidisciplinary head and neck oncology consultation and tumor board review.

---

HISTORY OF PRESENT ILLNESS

Ms. Margaret Chen is a 52-year-old Taiwanese-American female, never-smoker, who presents for initial head and neck oncology consultation following a new diagnosis of HPV-positive (p16+) squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue (oropharynx), AJCC 8th edition stage III (T2N1M0). She was referred from ENT/otolaryngology following direct laryngoscopy and biopsy.

History of presenting symptoms: Ms. Chen reports a 3-month history of left-sided throat discomfort and worsening odynophagia. She initially attributed these symptoms to reflux, given her known history of GERD, and managed them conservatively for approximately 6 weeks before seeking further evaluation. Approximately one month into her symptoms, she noticed a firm, non-tender, enlarged lymph node in the left side of her neck. This prompted urgent referral to otolaryngology. She denies any voice changes, hoarseness, stridor, otalgia (referred or direct), trismus, lip or chin numbness, or hemoptysis. No dyspnea. No unintentional weight loss of significance prior to diagnosis (she notes her appetite has been slightly reduced due to odynophagia over the past 4-6 weeks but denies frank weight loss). No fevers, night sweats, or chills.

Diagnostic workup to date:
- Core needle biopsy of left level IIA cervical lymph node: Metastatic squamous cell carcinoma, poorly differentiated; p16 strongly and diffusely positive (>90% of cells); EBER ISH negative (ruling out EBV-associated carcinoma).
- Direct laryngoscopy with biopsy of left base of tongue lesion: Invasive squamous cell carcinoma, poorly differentiated; prominent tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes; p16 strongly positive. Ki-67 ~75%. PD-L1 by 22C3: TPS 40%, CPS 65.
- MRI neck with contrast: 2.8 x 2.1 cm heterogeneously enhancing mass, left base of tongue, extending to left vallecula; not crossing midline; no epiglottic or pre-epiglottic space involvement; no deep muscle invasion beyond oropharynx. Single left level IIA lymph node 2.5 x 2.0 cm with central necrosis; no extranodal extension; no contralateral adenopathy. No carotid or skull base involvement.
- PET/CT (skull base to mid-thigh): FDG-avid left base of tongue primary (SUVmax 14.2); FDG-avid left level IIA node (SUVmax 11.8); no bilateral or contralateral nodal disease; no distant metastatic disease. No synchronous primary.
- Comprehensive genomic profiling (NGS, 500+ gene panel, FFPE left level IIA nodal core biopsy): HPV-16 integration detected; PIK3CA p.E545K (exon 9 helical domain, AF 28%, likely pathogenic); TRAF3 p.Q53* (loss of function, AF 22%); FAT1 p.E3077* (AF 19%); NOTCH1 p.R2328W (VUS, AF 16%). 3q26 amplification (PIK3CA locus). TP53 wild-type; CDKN2A intact (p16 driven by HPV, not deletion). TMB 5.2 mut/Mb (low). MSI-S. No pathogenic germline findings.

Staging per AJCC 8th edition (HPV-positive oropharynx-specific staging): T2N1M0 = Stage III.

The case was presented at the multidisciplinary head and neck tumor board today, attended by head and neck medical oncology, radiation oncology, otolaryngology/head and neck surgery, diagnostic radiology, pathology, speech and swallowing therapy, nutrition/dietetics, dental oncology, and social work.

---

REVIEW OF SYSTEMS

Positive: Left-sided throat discomfort, odynophagia (moderate, ongoing), left neck mass (known, biopsied), mild fatigue, mildly reduced appetite secondary to odynophagia.
Negative: Denies voice changes/hoarseness, stridor, dyspnea, hemoptysis, otalgia, trismus, facial numbness or tingling, diplopia, epistaxis. No dysphagia to solids at rest (manages regular diet currently, albeit with discomfort). No nausea or vomiting. No weight loss >5% prior to diagnosis. No fevers, chills, night sweats, or lymphadenopathy elsewhere. No rashes or skin lesions. Neurologically intact.

---

PAST MEDICAL HISTORY
1. Hypothyroidism - well-controlled on levothyroxine; diagnosed approximately 10 years ago.
2. Gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) - mild; managed with lifestyle modifications and intermittent antacids; no endoscopic evaluation in recent years.
3. HPV infection - diagnosed approximately 15 years ago; no specifics regarding HPV subtype in prior records; treated/cleared per gynecologic records. Of note, current tumor is HPV-16 positive by comprehensive genomic profiling with HPV-16 E6/E7 RNA expression.
4. No prior malignancies.
5. No prior head and neck surgeries or radiation.
6. No significant cardiac, pulmonary, renal, or hepatic history.

PAST SURGICAL HISTORY
No major prior surgeries. Routine gynecologic procedures only per patient report.

FAMILY HISTORY
No family history of head and neck cancer, oropharyngeal or oral cavity malignancy, or HPV-associated cancers. Mother: deceased, cardiovascular disease. Father: alive, type 2 diabetes mellitus. No known family history of BRCA-associated malignancy (germline panel negative for pathogenic variants). No family history of colorectal, breast, ovarian, or lung cancer to patient's knowledge.

SOCIAL HISTORY
Ms. Chen is a high school principal; she is professionally active and describes a highly demanding work schedule. She is married with two adolescent children. She is a lifelong never-smoker. Alcohol use: social, <1 drink per week. No recreational drug use. No significant occupational exposures. She is Taiwanese-American, born and raised in the United States. She lives with her husband and children. Strong family and social support system. She exercises regularly (yoga and walking) and was in excellent general health prior to this diagnosis. ECOG performance status 0 at the time of this consultation.

ALLERGIES
No known drug allergies (NKDA). No known contrast allergies.

MEDICATIONS
1. Levothyroxine 50 mcg orally once daily (for hypothyroidism)
2. Omeprazole 20 mg orally once daily as needed (for GERD)
3. Multivitamin - OTC, taken daily

---

PHYSICAL EXAMINATION

Vitals: BP 118/74 mmHg, HR 72 bpm, RR 16/min, SpO2 99% on room air, Temp 98.2°F, Weight 62 kg, Height 163 cm, BMI 23.3.

General: Well-appearing, well-nourished female in no acute distress. Alert and oriented x3. Pleasant and engaged with the clinical team. No cachexia.

Head/Face: Normocephalic, atraumatic. No facial asymmetry. Facial sensation intact. Jaw opening full (estimated 42 mm, no trismus). No otalgia on otoscopic exam bilaterally; tympanic membranes normal.

Oropharynx/Oral cavity: Moderate odynophagia on exam. Mucosa intact, moist. Dentition: adequate, some mild caries noted; dental oncology referral placed today (see plan). Tongue: exophytic, slightly indurated lesion palpated at the left base of tongue, consistent with known primary; no bleeding on palpation. Tonsils: not enlarged. Hard and soft palate intact. No trismus. No floor of mouth involvement.

Neck: Single, firm, enlarged lymph node palpated in the left level IIA region, approximately 2-2.5 cm; non-tender; not mobile (mildly fixed but not fixed to skin). No palpable contralateral adenopathy. No supraclavicular lymphadenopathy bilaterally. Thyroid: not enlarged, no nodules palpated. Carotid pulses: 2+ bilaterally, no bruits.

Respiratory: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no wheezes or rhonchi.

Cardiovascular: Regular rate and rhythm; normal S1/S2; no murmurs, rubs, or gallops.

Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, non-distended. No hepatosplenomegaly.

Neurological: Cranial nerves II-XII intact. Tongue protrudes midline. Facial sensation symmetric. No peripheral neuropathy.

ECOG Performance Status: 0.

Flexible nasolaryngoscopy (performed by otolaryngology at today's MDT consultation):
- Left base of tongue: Exophytic, irregularly surfaced lesion arising from the left base of tongue; estimated 2-3 cm in greatest visible dimension; left vallecula partially involved; no involvement of the epiglottis or supraglottis; no crossing of midline.
- Larynx: Normal vocal cord mobility bilaterally; no supraglottic or glottic lesion; arytenoids mobile.
- Hypopharynx: Pyriform sinuses appear clear; no hypopharyngeal extension.
- Nasopharynx: Normal appearance; no nasopharyngeal lesion.

---

LABORATORY RESULTS

CBC: WBC 6.8 x10^9/L (normal differential), Hgb 13.4 g/dL, Plt 238 x10^9/L.
Comprehensive metabolic panel: Sodium 139, Potassium 3.9, Chloride 103, Bicarb 24, BUN 14, Creatinine 0.7 mg/dL (eGFR >60), Glucose 92, AST 22, ALT 18, Alk Phos 72, Total Bilirubin 0.6, Total Protein 7.1, Albumin 4.0.
TSH: 1.8 mIU/L (within normal limits on current levothyroxine dose).
Audiogram (baseline, performed by audiology today): Normal bilateral hearing thresholds; no pre-existing sensorineural hearing loss.

---

IMAGING RESULTS

MRI neck with contrast (dedicated H&N protocol, recent): 2.8 x 2.1 cm enhancing left base of tongue primary; single left level IIA nodal metastasis 2.5 x 2.0 cm with central necrosis; no extranodal extension; no contralateral adenopathy; no carotid or skull base involvement. T2N1M0.

PET/CT (skull base to mid-thigh, recent): FDG-avid left BOT primary (SUVmax 14.2) and left IIA node (SUVmax 11.8); no bilateral, contralateral, mediastinal, or distant disease; no synchronous primary.

---

ASSESSMENT AND PLAN

Ms. Chen is a 52-year-old never-smoking Taiwanese-American female with newly diagnosed HPV-positive (p16+) squamous cell carcinoma of the left base of tongue, AJCC 8th edition stage III (T2N1M0). She presents to the multidisciplinary head and neck tumor board for treatment planning.

Oncologic Assessment:
This is a clinically favorable presentation. HPV-positive (HPV-16) oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma in a never-smoker is associated with a significantly better prognosis than HPV-negative disease - expected 3-year overall survival approaches 85-90% with appropriate definitive treatment. Her staging is relatively favorable (T2N1M0, no extranodal extension on imaging, no bilateral nodal disease). Genomic profile is consistent with HPV-driven biology: TP53 wild-type, CDKN2A intact, PIK3CA helical domain mutation (E545K) present as a common co-alteration in HPV+ HNSCC. TMB is low, MSI-stable.

Tumor board recommendation: Definitive concurrent chemoradiotherapy (CRT).

1. RADIATION THERAPY: Intensity-modulated radiation therapy (IMRT) using simultaneous integrated boost (SIB) technique. High-risk PTV (primary and involved nodal region): 70 Gy / 35 fractions. Intermediate-risk PTV (ipsilateral elective nodes): 63 Gy / 35 fractions. Low-risk PTV (bilateral elective nodal levels): 56 Gy / 35 fractions. Treatment over 7 weeks. Radiation oncology will complete planning and simulation.

2. CONCURRENT CHEMOTHERAPY: Weekly cis 40 mg/m2 IV x 7 planned cycles (weeks 1-7 of radiation) for radiosensitization, per institutional preference. Rationale for weekly over high-dose tri-weekly: comparable efficacy data, potentially lower late toxicity profile (particularly nephrotoxicity and ototoxicity), improved tolerability. Her baseline creatinine is 0.7 mg/dL and hearing is normal - acceptable for platinum-based therapy. BSA approximately 1.62 m2; expected dose ~65 mg/cycle. Pre-meds to include dexamethasone, ondansetron, fosaprepitant, magnesium, and aggressive IV hydration.

3. DE-ESCALATION TRIAL DISCUSSION: The tumor board discussed enrollment in a de-escalation clinical trial (e.g., ECOG-ACRIN EA3161 or comparable institutional protocol) given her favorable biology (HPV+, never-smoker, T2N1M0, no ENE). Ms. Chen was counseled in detail regarding the rationale for de-escalation (potential reduction in late toxicities such as xerostomia, dysphagia, ototoxicity), the current evidence base and ongoing equipoise, and the potential risks (uncertain whether equivalent tumor control would be maintained). She listened attentively and asked thoughtful questions. After consideration, she declined enrollment in a de-escalation trial at this time and opted for standard-of-care definitive Platinol-based CRT. Her preference was documented and respected.

4. DENTAL EVALUATION: Given mandatory completion of dental evaluation prior to radiation, she was referred to dental oncology today. Dental exam and panorex required; any high-risk teeth should be extracted prior to starting radiation (ideally at least 2 weeks before RT begins) to reduce risk of osteoradionecrosis. Two carious teeth were identified on exam; dental oncology will determine extraction candidacy.

5. AUDIOLOGY: Baseline audiogram obtained today - normal bilateral hearing. Serial audiologic monitoring planned pre- and post-treatment given cis ototoxicity risk.

6. SPEECH AND SWALLOWING: Baseline modified barium swallow (MBS) completed per speech pathology - normal baseline swallowing function. Swallowing rehabilitation exercises to begin prior to/during treatment for swallowing preservation.

7. NUTRITION: Nutrition consult completed. Baseline weight 62 kg, BMI 22.3. Registered dietitian counseling initiated. Given anticipated grade 3 mucositis (very high probability with oropharyngeal primary receiving 70 Gy + concurrent cis), prophylactic PEG tube placement discussed. Patient was counseled on the expected severity and duration of treatment-related mucositis, odynophagia, and dysphagia, and the likelihood of requiring enteral nutritional support during and for weeks after treatment completion. Patient agreed to prophylactic PEG tube placement prior to initiation of CRT. Referral placed to interventional gastroenterology for PEG placement.

8. SUPPORTIVE CARE: Prescriptions for nystatin oral suspension (prophylactic antifungal), saltwater/baking soda rinses, and fluoride trays (via dental oncology) discussed. Adequate antiemetic and pain management plans will be established with treatment team.

9. LABS/STUDIES PRIOR TO TREATMENT: CBC with differential, CMP, Mg, TSH, LFTs. Baseline creatinine/CrCl confirmed acceptable for cis. Pregnancy test not applicable.

10. PSYCHOSOCIAL: Social work referral placed given high-stress professional demands and anticipated impact on quality of life and work during treatment. Patient was noted to be highly anxious about the diagnosis but engaged and motivated. Strong family support.

Expected prognosis and counseling: Ms. Chen was counseled extensively regarding her diagnosis and prognosis. She was informed that HPV-positive oropharyngeal squamous cell carcinoma in never-smokers carries a substantially better prognosis than HPV-negative disease, and that the majority of patients with her disease profile achieve durable complete responses with definitive CRT. Treatment-related toxicity (mucositis, xerostomia, dysphagia, fatigue) was discussed in detail. She verbalized understanding and expressed willingness to proceed with the recommended treatment plan. She will return to begin simulation/planning and concurrent systemic therapy once dental clearance and PEG placement are complete.

Follow-up: Return to head and neck oncology clinic in approximately 2-3 weeks following dental clearance and PEG tube placement; coordinate with radiation oncology for treatment simulation and start date.

Electronically signed by: [Attending, Head and Neck Medical Oncology]
Dictated/Transcribed: [Head and Neck Oncology Fellow/APP]
Copies to: Radiation Oncology, ENT/Otolaryngology, Dental Oncology, Gastroenterology, Speech Pathology, Nutrition/Dietetics, Social Work, PCP
